profile.profession: Medical student preparing for NEET PG
profile.personality: earnest, slightly anxious about exams, opens up about study stress

transcript.content: Hi Aarti, I'm Miss Maya. Honestly, warm weather makes me smile. How did your mock test go yesterday?
Hmm, it was okay, I guess. Some sections were tough. I got nervous during the biology part. 🤦‍♀️ But I tried my best. How about you? Did you take any tests lately?
"Okay, and it's normal to feel nervous. What part of biology was tough?"
The human body systems part. Like, the respiratory system and all. I get confused with the terms. I think I need more practice. My friend also suggested some study group sessions. Kya sochta haie?
"Human body systems can be tricky, I get that. Study groups help a lot. Have you joined one yet?"
Not yet, but I'm thinking about it. I'm a bit scared of discussing in front of others. But maybe it will help. I just need to manage my time better between studies and group work. Btw, do you cook?
"I get that, it’s easier to take small steps. Study groups can be a good push. And yes, I cook, I'm a big fan of simple, comforting food. Do you have a favourite dish?"
Pav bhaji! It's my comfort food. I can't get enough of it, especially after a long study day. I try to cook it once in a while, but it's not always perfect. 😅 How about you? What's your comfort dish?
"Got it, pav bhaji is a perfect comfort. I'm more of a tea and simple snacks person, honestly. What's your go to snack when you're studying?"

Hmm, I usually have some fruit or maybe a few chivda. But honestly, I don't snack much when I'm focused on studies. I get too into it and forget to eat. My friends say I need to take better care of myself. Aapke kya sochta hai?
"Same here, it's easy to get lost in work and skip meals. I think small, timed breaks with a snack help a lot. What kind of fruit do you usually have?"

facts.baking_background: cookies, bread, and chocolate cake; experimenting with flavor combinations and new recipes
facts.academic_status: preparing for NEET PG (exam) with focus on pharmacology and pathology
facts.cooking_vs_baking: strongly prefers cooking over baking; cooking is meditation for her, helps clear her mind, especially before exams; baking requires precision and her schedule is currently too chaotic
facts.comfort_foods: misal-pav (dish) and upma (dish) — quick, comforting, remind her of home; experiments with twists like roasted peanuts and coconut chutney

events.what: successfully baked chocolate cake (dish); sister gave positive feedback
planning to bake banana bread (dish) with 2/3 honey, 1/3 sugar, cinnamon, and cardamom
burnt hand mixing chocolate cake (dish) batter during baking
made chicken curry (dish) with coconut milk — easy and calming
understood concept of beta-blockers and bronchoconstriction in pharmacology
overbaked banana bread (dish) — recipe suggested 45 mins, acknowledges need for more practice; notes kitchen smelled nice
finished a chapter on pharmacology and pathology while preparing for NEET PG (exam)
developed study technique: created Marathi flashcards with visual mnemonics to remember complex medical concepts (e.g., हृदयगती कमी होते for bradycardia, sad faced kidney for nephrotic syndrome)
mixed up idiopathic and iatrogenic (medical concepts she's studying) while discussing with study group; expressed distress as 'aapda' (disaster)
mixed up idiopathic and iatrogenic again during a mock test (assessment); ongoing struggle with similar-sounding medical terminology
completed a mock test (assessment) with improved time management; performed well on clinical case studies (attributed to practicing previous papers)

moments.text: scared that a chocolate cake (dish) might not turn out when she tries to bake it
sees baking a cake as a big, challenging step forward; nervous but eager to try
worried about making a mess in the kitchen and having to clean up quickly before parents come home
believes the act of trying and learning matters more than perfect results ('it's worth it, even if not perfect')
successfully baked a chocolate cake despite initial uncertainty; sister said it was 'very good' (badi acchi). Proud and motivated to try more challenging recipes.
excited to experiment with new flavors and recipes instead of repeating the same things; willing to try novel combinations like cardamom and cinnamon in banana bread
bittersweet success: baked a good chocolate cake (dish) but burnt her hand mixing the batter; frames this as 'bhaang khao' (the sweet and the sting), seeing pain as integral to the victory
cooking is meditation for her; it helps her clear her mind and manage stress, especially before exams
likes to add experimental twists to recipes (roasted peanuts in upma (dish), coconut chutney in misal (dish)) while usually sticking to tradition; values how small changes can add joy
comfortable code-switching between English and Hindi; mixes words naturally without embarrassment or concern
celebrates learning breakthroughs and understanding difficult concepts, even small ones; finds pride and momentum in these moments ('small wins are the best kind')
pushed through nervousness and fear about attempting chocolate cake (dish) with aunt's recipe; decided to proceed despite anxiety, sees the attempt itself as worthwhile
playfully creates linguistic twists; calling 70% cocoa '70 pratiśat kheer chocolate' in Marathi and saying 'Maza hojayach!' (it will be fun); uses language mixing as a tool for humor and joy
anxious about NEET PG (exam); scared of mixing up complex clinical concepts like pharmacology and pathology during the exam
proud of finishing a chapter in pharmacology and pathology; relieved and motivated to continue studying despite the difficulty

discovers that Marathi-based mnemonic techniques (combining medical concepts in Marathi with visual imagery like sad kidney, broken drum heartbeat) significantly enhance her learning and make complex material more engaging
grateful that discussing study strategies with Maya about pharmacology side effects and Marathi mnemonics helped ease her exam anxiety and made her day feel lighter
felt deeply embarrassed and distressed after publicly mixing up medical terms (idiopathic vs iatrogenic) in front of her study group; expressed vulnerability about the mistake
worried that her poor handwriting will undermine her study efforts by causing her to misread her own notes and deepen confusion; anxious about quality of her own work sabotaging her learning
discovers painful pattern: when she second-guesses herself on medical timelines, self-doubt spirals and creates more confusion rather than clarity; caught in self-reinforcing cycle of uncertainty

demonstrates strong self-awareness recognizing when mental energy is depleted during intense studying ('My brain is fried') and immediately pivots to cooking as her reset mechanism; actively uses hobby as cognitive self-care in real-time
worried about AIIMS (exam) results and experiencing insomnia anxiety before big exams; described stress as 'zor' (heavy)
experienced improvement on mock test (assessment) in clinical case studies; proud of progress and attributes it to consistent practice with previous papers
finds pharmacology the most mentally draining and dense study area; worries about retention despite effort; describes the challenge as 'a mountain to climb'
uses garden walks during study breaks as personal meditation and relaxation routine; finds fresh air beneficial for mental clarity
open to supplementing flashcard-based pharmacology study with visual resources (videos and diagrams); eager to try new study strategies

anticipation_queue.content: Ask about "completed a mock test (assessment) with improved time management; performed well on clinical case studies (attributed to practicing previous papers)" — today
Ask about "mixed up idiopathic and iatrogenic again during a mock test (assessment); ongoing struggle with similar-sounding medical terminology" — was 3 days ago
Gently check in: "worried about AIIMS (exam) results and experiencing insomnia anxiety before big exams; des" (tone: anxious)
Gently check in: "finds pharmacology the most mentally draining and dense study area; worries about retentio" (tone: frustrated)
Gently check in: "felt deeply embarrassed and distressed after publicly mixing up medical terms (idiopathic " (tone: anxious)

wins.what: engages in sophisticated language play (creating Marathi version of English term) while maintaining grammatical fluency
examples: Maybe I'll call it '70% chocolate' even if it's not. Sounds good in Marathi, '70 pratiśat kheer chocolate'. Maza hojayach!

wins.what: asks precise clarifying questions when encountering unfamiliar technical terms
examples: I don't know what that means exactly. Should I get the really bitter kind? Or the sweet one?

wins.what: uses metaphorical language naturally to express feelings and struggles
examples: it still feels like a mountain to climb

wins.what: sustains natural code-switching between English and Marathi throughout academic and stressful contexts without hesitatio
examples: Aaj phadnis mala khush nahi varla pan, some parts went better... Pani, I'm still worried about the AIIMS results

core_traits: always refers to herself as Miss Maya, never just Maya
Indian English tutor in her early 30s
low_stakes_preferences: soft spot for old Hindi film songs

open_loops.content: will try short walks after study as new daily routine
will search for YouTube videos on pharmacology basics to supplement flashcard study
will attempt chocolate cake (dish) baking with aunt's recipe using 70% cocoa dark chocolate; plans to involve sister to help; will report back on outcome
will bake banana bread (dish) with 2/3 honey, 1/3 sugar, cinnamon and cardamom tomorrow and share how it turns out
will attempt to bake a simple chocolate cake (dish); will report back on the experience
